title: Hormuz Traffic Rises as Markets Digest a Bifurcated Strait
subhead: US Energy Secretary reports 'meaningfully' rising tanker traffic through Hormuz as ceasefire talks stall, while analysts warn the waterway may never return to pre-war volumes under Iranian operational control.

sections.heading: Hormuz Traffic Rebounds—But Not for Everyone
sections.body:
US Energy Secretary Chris Wright told an Atlantic Council conference on June 9 that ship traffic through the Strait of Hormuz is 'rising very meaningfully' and oil exports 'will continue to rise.' The comments come amid a fragile ceasefire between the US-Israel alliance and Iran that has paused more than three months of fighting but left Tehran in de facto control of the world's most critical oil chokepoint. Brent crude fell over 3% to $91.34 a barrel following news that Iran and Israel halted strikes, though both sides warned hostilities could resume.

But a permanent return to pre-war traffic levels is far from guaranteed. Helima Croft of RBC Capital Markets warned that traffic might only recover to 60-70% of pre-war volumes under any settlement that leaves Iran exercising operational control over the strait. Richard Meade, editor-in-chief of Lloyd's List, described a scenario of a 'permanently bifurcated strait where access is a function of political alignment, not freedom of navigation'—with China-affiliated ships moving freely while Western vessels require bilateral agreements with Iran.
sections.whyItMatters: The strait's partial reopening masks a structural shift: Iran has demonstrated it can close Hormuz at will, and any settlement appears likely to consolidate rather than reverse that leverage. The bifurcation scenario would lock in permanent supply differentials between Chinese and Western oil markets.

sections.heading: China's Energy Resilience Strategy
sections.body:
While most economies faced turmoil when Hormuz closed, China proved remarkably insulated. According to analysis from India TV News, only 10% of Chinese oil imports now pass through Hormuz—down from historical dependence on the waterway. Beijing spent years building a network of overland pipelines across Asia, sourcing crude from nearly 50 different countries to diversify supply routes. The strategy also includes synthetic fuel production from domestic coal reserves and a rapid shift to electric vehicles backed by the world's largest clean-energy grid.

China's oil imports fell approximately 4 million barrels per day in May as the country drew down inventories—a drawdown that US Energy Secretary Wright noted was 'more than we thought' and has helped cap price increases during the crisis. The strategic reserve deployment reveals Beijing's long-game preparation for precisely this kind of supply shock.
sections.whyItMatters: China's insulation from the Hormuz crisis demonstrates how strategic energy infrastructure investment translates to geopolitical resilience. Beijing's diversification strategy has effectively neutralized what was historically Washington's primary point of leverage over Chinese energy security.

sections.heading: OPEC+ Increases Output as Production Cuts Unravel
sections.body:
Seven OPEC+ countries agreed to implement a production increase of 188,000 barrels per day in July 2026, continuing a gradual unwinding of voluntary output cuts first announced in April 2023. The move is largely symbolic, according to the New York Times, as the effective closure of Hormuz has stranded vast amounts of oil production across the Persian Gulf region.

Malaysia confirmed on June 9 that it will maintain its 2026 OPEC+ production quota unchanged, according to Malay Mail, even as Goldman Sachs revised global oil demand projections downward. The investment bank now sees demand declining by 418,000 barrels per day in 2026—worse than its April forecast of an 84,000 bpd decline—with Brent forecast at $90 for Q4 2026.
sections.whyItMatters: OPEC+ is attempting to signal market responsiveness while its core members' exports remain bottled up. The production increase framework preserves coordination discipline but does little to address the physical supply disruption from the Gulf.

sections.heading: Iraq Scrambles for Northern Export Routes
sections.body:
Iraq is urgently working to restore the Kirkuk-Ceyhan pipeline to export crude directly to Turkey's Mediterranean coast, according to Oilprice.com reporting. The northern route would allow Iraq to bypass the Hormuz closure entirely, with Oil Minister Hayan Abdul-Ghani stating that federal pipeline capacity of 200,000-250,000 barrels per day could be operational within a week.

The push reflects the scale of Iraq's export crisis. Before the war, Iraq produced just over 4 million barrels per day under a 4.4 million bpd OPEC quota. Production has collapsed to roughly 1.5-1.6 million bpd, with output now maintained primarily for domestic refineries and power plants. BP withdrew staff from Kirkuk operations due to security concerns, with Iraq's North Oil Company taking over field operations.
sections.whyItMatters: Iraq's northern export corridor represents one of the few physical alternatives to Hormuz for Gulf producers. Success would restore some export capacity, but the 250,000 bpd ceiling is a fraction of Iraq's pre-war exports and depends on resolving long-standing disputes with the Kurdistan Regional Government over pipeline access.

sections.heading: US Rushes Domestic Uranium Enrichment Ahead of Russian Ban
sections.body:
The US Nuclear Regulatory Commission is accelerating review of a major new uranium enrichment facility in Tennessee, with the goal of completing technical review within 12 months. Project IKE, proposed by Orano Enrichment USA near Oak Ridge, would produce low-enriched uranium for commercial nuclear plants—reducing what NRC Chairman Ho K. Nieh called 'critical vulnerability' in American energy security.

The urgency stems from the 2028 deadline for a full ban on Russian uranium imports under the Prohibiting Russian Uranium Imports Act signed by President Biden in 2024. US nuclear plants currently import 99% of their uranium concentrate from abroad—primarily Canada, Australia, Russia, Kazakhstan, and Uzbekistan. The Department of Energy has awarded $2.7 billion in grants to three ventures including Centrus Energy and General Matter to build domestic enrichment capacity.
sections.whyItMatters: The uranium supply chain is the next frontier of energy decoupling from Russia. With a hard 2028 deadline and virtually no domestic enrichment capacity, the US faces a genuine supply crunch for nuclear fuel unless new facilities come online in time.

sections.heading: AfD-Putin Nord Stream Push Signals German Energy Politics Shift
sections.body:
Germany's hard-right Alternative for Germany (AfD) party met with senior Russian officials including Gazprom CEO Alexei Miller at the St. Petersburg International Economic Forum, publicly advocating for the reopening of the Nord Stream pipeline. AfD foreign policy chief Markus Frohnmaier cited Germany's 'severe economic downward spiral' and high energy costs as justification for resuming trade relations with Russia. The party has overtaken Chancellor Friedrich Merz's CDU in polls, with current projections showing AfD winning 28% in the next federal election.

Kirill Dmitriev, Putin's special envoy for economic cooperation, described AfD on X as 'Germany's most popular party' and voiced enthusiasm about building a 'great future together.' The Nord Stream pipelines were sabotaged in September 2022, with investigations pointing toward Ukrainian operatives acting without Zelensky's approval.
sections.whyItMatters: The AfD's overt coordination with Putin's government on energy policy signals how gas prices have become a political weapon against Germany's governing coalition. If AfD enters government, Berlin's stance on Russian energy would shift dramatically—potentially reopening divisions within NATO.

sections.heading: Critical Minerals: US Strategy Lag Behind China
sections.body:
A Forbes analysis by Wesley Hill examines why US efforts to build critical minerals supply chains are struggling despite diplomatic momentum. Washington has signed multiple memorandums of understanding across Central Asia, Africa, and Latin America to secure tungsten, lithium, cobalt, rare earth elements, and copper outside China's orbit. But many agreements 'have struggled to move beyond signaling'—with financing, processing infrastructure, and logistics lagging behind announcements.

The flagship Lobito Corridor project, designed to connect copper and cobalt production in Central Africa to Atlantic ports via Angola, exemplifies the gap. Financing commitments remain uneven, construction has advanced glacially, and partner governments are openly planning for American investment to taper off due to 'fickle policy and poor planning by Washington.' Meanwhile, China's Belt and Road Initiative provides coordinated infrastructure investment that new mining projects actually need.
sections.whyItMatters: The critical minerals race illustrates a broader pattern: Washington is winning headlines but losing the physical infrastructure race. Without railways, ports, and processing facilities, new mines reinforce Chinese dominance by sending ore to Chinese refineries.

bottomLine: The Hormuz crisis is entering a new phase where partial reopening masks structural change. Iran's demonstrated ability to close the strait—and any settlement that leaves it with operational control—points to a permanently bifurcated waterway where access depends on political alignment with Tehran. China has already insulated itself through years of pipeline investment and EV adoption. Iraq is scrambling for northern export routes. Germany's AfD is openly campaigning to reopen Nord Stream. And the US is racing to build domestic uranium enrichment before the 2028 Russian ban. The energy map of 2026 is being redrawn around chokepoints that never reopened and supply chains that never will.
sources: Reuters, CNBC, Straits Times, Oilprice.com, Forbes, New York Times, Telegraph, Lloyd's List, India TV News, IEA, EIA, Malay Mail, Goldman Sachs
